description: RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3 (ECMAScript Number::toString) serialization vectors. Every {value, canonical} pair is consumed by BOTH the Python (canonical.py) and TypeScript (canonical.ts) contract legs; scripts/check_workflow_contract_fixtures.py fails if either language drifts from these bytes. Verified against Node/V8 native String() on the exact IEEE754 double bit pattern of every value (200k-vector fuzz run, zero mismatches) — see the WS1-follow-up (float canonicalization) execution log.

vectors.note: RFC 8785 canonical example — 0.1 has no exact binary representation; shortest round-trip repr must still be "0.1".
ES Number::toString exponential threshold: n=22 > 21, forces exponential notation.
One below the exponential threshold: n=21 stays in fixed (integer) notation.
Largest double strictly below 1e21 with maximal significant digits, still fixed notation (n=21).
ES Number::toString small-exponential threshold: n=-6 forces exponential notation.
One above the small-exponential threshold: n=-5 stays fixed ("0.000001").
Integral-valued float must serialize without a decimal point ("2", not "2.0") — the WS2b StartRun bug.
IEEE754 negative zero serializes as "0" (no sign) per ECMA-262 Number::toString.
Positive zero serializes as "0".
A typical fractional workflow number input default.
Negative fractional input.
Integral float with trailing zeros in fixed notation.
The smallest positive subnormal double (Number.MIN_VALUE).
The largest finite double (Number.MAX_VALUE).
Negative Number.MAX_VALUE.
1/3 — a long non-terminating shortest-round-trip digit string.
Negative fractional value with a terminating decimal.
A common non-round fractional literal.
Small integral float.
Small negative integral float.
Half-integer fractional value — a realistic StartRun number input.
A physical-constant-scale value forcing exponential notation with multiple significant digits.
A small fractional value inside the fixed-notation floor.
Negative counterpart of the above.
An ordinary multi-digit fractional value.
Integral float with one trailing zero.
A large integral float below the 1e21 exponential threshold, exercising many trailing zeros vs. significant digits.
